speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is May 17, 1995. And we're doing the Shoah interview. And the interviewee is Mike Popik. And the interviewer is Adelle Chabelski. And we're in Los Angeles, California, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Adelle Chabelski, and I'm interviewing Mike Popik today, May 17, 1995, in Los Angeles, California-- or is it Beverly Hills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles. And we're doing the interview in English. Mike, could you introduce yourself and tell us where and when you were born, and if you could, spell your name for us please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mike Popik. I was born in May 11, 1931, Levice, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Levice, Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levice is about 100 kilometer from Bratislava, close to the Hungarian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-P-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at home, I just spelled it M-I-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is William Popik, and my mother's name was Frida Popik [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other children in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a brother, Andras Popik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 16 and 1/2. And my younger brother, Gabi, 2 and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your town, the city, Levice, that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a small city in Slovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably 18,000 or 19,000 people and 2,000 Jews. It was a nice city, and we had a fairly good life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in school already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I was in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what grade did you finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just elementary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, I finished. It was actually a Hungarian school. Because as you know, in 1938, this part of Slovakia was given to the Hungarians. So I finished few grades in Slovakian schools and five grades-- yeah, five grades in the Hungarian schools. So I finished seven, almost eight grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish education, Hebrew classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not Hebrew. Well, we had the Jewish education once a week when our Gentile friends had their religion. Ours, we went to a Jewish school just for-- I think, for two or three hours a week. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family an observant Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were observant. Not too religious, but observant. Shabbas was Shabbas, and holidays were holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you observed all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we observed all the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept kosher till 1941. After '41, it was impossible to keep kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a partner in a truck-- in a trucking company with two Gentile partners. The three owners, they had five trucks, and they were also mechanics and the drivers. So we did all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the languages spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spoke in Hungarian and Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: And some German. Some German. No Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- you were quite little, but do you remember what the relations were between the Jewish population, the Jews and the non-Jews in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, of course. Very good. We were only two, maybe three Jewish families in the entire street where we lived. And we had very good relationships with the Gentile population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's partners were-- were non-Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --so they must have gotten along very well too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say they were friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some of them were very good friends, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any incidents of antisemitism that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, only doing the Hungarian occupation. Not in the Slovak or Czechoslovakian occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was that, and what do you remember? What is it exactly you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember is that one day, my mother and my father told us that the Hungarians are occupying our city. And my mother was born in Budapest. So she was waiting for the Hungarians. And in the morning, the day the Czechoslovakian army moved out from our city, and they throw garbage all over with their tanks to disturb the Hungarian army to parade into our city, Levice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- we liked the Czechoslovakian army. We were-- we couldn't believe that they are moving out. But of course, as a little boy, so we waiting it for some-- for the changes. So the Hungarians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I think it was November-- end of November 1938. I was seven years old. And when the Hungarian army came into the town, it was a terrible disappointment. They looked differently than the-- than the Czechoslovakian army. They were very poorly dressed. But the main thing is they were singing anti-Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] slogans and anti-Jewish songs. So it was a terrible disappointment to the Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand the songs or the slogans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, because we spoke Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were some of those slogans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just dirty Jews, you are going to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we couldn't believe it. It was a drastic change. At night, a bus came to our city, a huge bus. I remember the bus because I never saw such a big bus before. Those were the Hungarian-- the representatives of the Hungarian government. And they went to lay flowers on the tomb of the unknown soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are children. We ran after the car. We wanted to see, with my Gentile friends and Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the tomb of the unknown soldier, they were yelling those anti-Jewish slogans. So I just left my friends, and I ran home. And I was crying. And I told my father, my mother what did they-- what I heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the father tried to quiet the things down, that it's-- it's just some people that it doesn't have too much importance. And it really, the things went OK for the first couple months. But the difference was tremendous after the Czechoslovakians left. I wanted to ask you, do you think your father told you it wouldn't make a big difference because he wanted to comfort you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a child that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, obviously--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --troubled by it, or do you think he believed it didn't mean very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was just trying to comfort us. He saw the-- the disaster. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents talking either to-- to-- to the other children or to each other about what was going on in Germany, let's say, before '38, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we know very little. The truth is, we know very little. Because from the beginning, the first couple years were fairly OK. But in 1941, when they started the anti-Jewish laws, then our radios were taken away, and the news was pro-German, although the Hungarians directly did not attack the Jews. But the-- the children couldn't go to the gymnasiums. The-- all the-- the children couldn't go to study doctors and lawyers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So slowly, slowly, we adopted to this different and antisemitic life. And the Gentile population, they adapted also. Slowly, slowly they became anti-- antisemites. Most of them, if not all of them, but most of them. So they adapted in a negative way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Negative way. The teachers especially. Very few teachers were that they had-- they were passionate to us and with feelings. But most of the teachers were antisemites during the Hungarian rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did they do that because it was some kind of opportunity for them, or was it that they were afraid of the power?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was an opportunity. This is my feeling. My feeling, and then after war when I visited there, I asked my friends. And they said that it was because of the opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I know what I had in mind when I was asking opportunity. But can you explain? Is it that they were going to benefit in some way by being antisemitic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, benefit till we were there, they were benefiting for-- for their jobs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No question about it. And also, slowly, slowly, they pushed out the Jews from their positions. So business wise, they take over the-- slowly, slowly, the businesses. They took over the factories. Very slowly. Doctors. There were no Jewish children graduated as doctors. So they had the most opportunity-- sure, opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is your memory of all those years, your childhood years and war years? Is it pretty good, your-- your memory of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent memory, yeah. I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of details?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember all the details. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your childhood like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before was very pleasant, playing soccer, like all the Slovak or Hungarian children, playing with our Gentile friends. Going to-- when I was very little, I didn't have too much opportunities, but we-- we had a normal life. Normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. We traveled. I traveled and our family traveled because we had-- we had cars. But to travel with a train, if you traveled 20 kilometers, this was a big travel already. 20, up to 30 kilometers, it was a big travel. This was the travel for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there cultural activities in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. Sure, I belonged to Maccabi. To the-- but Maccabi, it was a sport club. It had nothing to do with Israel. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home, did your parents read newspapers, books? What were some of the activities in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Activities, going fishing, camping, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nice-- nice prewar European life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents interested in what was going on in other countries or outside the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were interested. But I think the news were traveling very slow. Most of them, most of the things we did not believe what is happening, because we just couldn't believe that those things that the Polish children-- they ran away from some camps, concentration camps, and ended up in our city, two of them, in 1942. They told us horror stories that even the rabbi, Saturday in the shul, told us that we shouldn't believe. Those are gypsy children, and the stories are just not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they gypsy children, or were they Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, those were Jewish-Polish children. But for some reason, the Hungarians and the Polish, they didn't like each other. For some reason, they called them gypsies. This is what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what these boys, what-- what they said they saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, those children, I went to-- to school together with them. And it was 1942 when those two children arrived in our city. And one Jewish family without children took them in. And they were-- they went to school with us. But when they arrived, they told us, when they already learned the Hungarian language, they told us that their family, the entire [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish population from their town, was herded to a synagogue, and they were burned alive. So it was very hard to believe. But if I remember, they were crying when they told us those stories. So we didn't believe them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were these boys' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember their names. They were given Hungarian names. Their original names, I don't remember. And we-- we just went to the same school. They were-- they were older than I was. We went to the same school, so I remember, they went to school with us, and they went back to Auschwitz with us and probably didn't even survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think they were when they arrived in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they arrived, they were children of 14, probably 14, 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you happen to remember what town they-- they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland, they came. They spoke Yiddish. Yiddish and probably-- and we-- we did not spoke Yiddish in our town. So communicate them from the beginning was very hard. But they learned the language very fast, and we played together with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you mention that there were about 2,000 Jewish families in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, 2,000 together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say most of those people were assimilated or observant Jews? How would you describe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were observant Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the town support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did the town? First of all, it was-- it was-- as I remember, it was a center of a community. It was a main city from surrounding small towns. So agriculture was booming, and some factories. As a matter of fact, all the factories I remember were-- belonged to Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the factories? What did they make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it was a sugar factory, a huge sugar factory. Like candies, candy factory, textile factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names of the these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the-- the candy factory belonged to Kleins, family Klein. Then the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the building supply, huge building supply, belonged to-- the Roth family, very rich. Schultz family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Deutch family. They were all-- they had factories. They had factories. Small, but very well doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the other-- the rest of the population support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had different-- they had all-- they were doctors and dental technicians and lawyers and-- and businesses and profession, mechanics and photographers, and everything that you can find between the-- the Gentile population, we had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the rabbi-- do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember. Rabbi-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- now I can't remember. But I-- I know his name. I just can't remember. I can see his face, even. I don't remember his name now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you think of it later--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, maybe later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if you could tell me. When he said not to believe these boys, why do you think he-- he said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what was the real reason. But I have a feeling, especially after the war, that all the rabbis were, all over, was almost the same story. They tried to minimize the-- the problems. They were probably told by the government officials to keep cool, to-- not to panic, to not to try and run away, to not to make stupid things. They did it very smart. Not the rabbis, but the people that give them the orders. And this is what the rabbis did everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think it was more to minimize rather than a kind of denial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Minimize, yes. Minimize. Maybe they knew much-- much more than we did, although I don't believe it. I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how the rabbis behaved when it became obvious or when-- when there were deportations? I'm sort of jumping ahead, but do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw only one-- I remember only the one rabbi from our temple. We had a beautiful, big temple there. And the rabbi and the cantor, they were deported, just like we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until the end--

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to stay together and to believe that everything will be OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rabbis, what were they saying at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably it was then too late. I-- this I don't know. But when we were taken, and they concentrated us in an army complex-- this was actually the-- not the ghetto. In the ghetto, those were designated streets for the Jewish population from the beginning, because Jews from the entire area came to Levice. We were the big city. And so they were streets designated only for Jews. From the ghetto-- that we were only one month in the ghetto-- we were taken to this huge army complex. And we were about over 2,000 there. So it was already very hard to communicate with people. I mean, we didn't know where are the people. And he had no leaders between us. So it was hard to know what was their thinking. But everybody said that we will be relocated deep in Hungaria in some work camps. And we believed till the last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was saying that you would be relocated, the Germans or Hungarians or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians and the Jews, that they are going to relocate us in working-- work camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was the ghetto established in Levice?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Levice, in May 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about the ghetto, was it isolated by streets, or were there actually some kind of boundaries, walls, or anything that was put up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just isolated streets. And between the-- between the-- the-- the front and the beginning of the street and end of the street was just barbed wire. And some Hungarian soldiers were watching it. And the people that were watching it were not really dressed as soldiers. They [? wore ?] civilian clothing. They had, I think, the Hungarian banner on that the Hungarian had, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the Arrow Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the Arrow Cross. In our city was no-- no functioning Arrow Cross. They were people belong to the Arrow Cross, and they were shouting this, dirty Jews. But was no official-- I don't remember that they were parading like the Arrow Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the barbed wire was put up for-- for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't leave those-- those streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that barbed wire did not exist before the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the ghettoization, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember-- do you-- do you remember how the beginning of the war affected you, and when was that? Was that in '39? Was it later? How did the war come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war came to me that, in 1938, the Czechoslovakian Army was mobilizing. My father was a sergeant in the Czechoslovakian Army, and he was mobilized. And I was very proud of him when I saw him all dressed up as a soldier. And we were-- they were taken to the train station. Everything moved by trains then. So I was happy, but I was also crying that the father left. And they went somewhere. I can't remember where they-- where they took them exactly because when he wrote to us, he had only army numbers, no addresses. So when the Hungarians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took over Levice, he was in the Czechoslovakian Army. So we were terrible concerned that the father is in the Czechoslovakian Army, and we are already in Hungaria. But three or four months later, we get orders that-- I mean, news that older parents-- I mean, fathers and children, that they left the Czechoslovakian Army. They are coming back, walking back from Czechoslovakia, and they are coming home. So every day, we ran to the Slovakian border, waiting for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran? You and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my brother, and the friends. We were running, waiting for them, because we were told that they are walking home. They didn't give them transportation. But one day they really arrived. And we were happy. Disappointed, but happy that they came home. And they came home, you know, very dirty looking. And because they were walking for so long, probably weeks, from somewhere in Slovakia. And they were-- even though my father was Slovakian, but they-- they didn't give them help in Slovakia because they were considered Hungarians. So. But they came home. And my father, because he was a diver, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if he was privileged, but he was driving even after, driving his trucks between Slovakia and Hungaria. So this way it started for me, the war. But we got used to it, and everything was OK. Even we got used to that-- that our friends are calling us dirty Jew. You know, day by day, it was tough. But the real war started for me when the Germans walked into my town. And this was in April, Pesach 1944.
